figures.name: Kamuy-huci (Ape-huci, the Hearth-Fire Grandmother)
figures.gloss: The fire that burns on every hearth, addressed as a venerable grandmother seated at the heart of the house. Eldest and most constant of the kamuy, she is the indispensable go-between: no prayer reaches any other kamuy except as she carries it up in the smoke, and no kamuy may be sent home without passing through her flame. She receives the first food, the first sake, and the dead.
figures.facet: Divinity as a switchboard, not a sovereign — she rules nothing, yet nothing reaches the gods without her, so the warmest, most domestic presence in the house is also its only gate to heaven. The great-mother reimagined as the patient relay of every word humans dare to send upward.
figures.provenance: Munro, 'Ainu Creed and Cult' (her primacy as intercessor is Munro's central observation); Batchelor; Philippi, 'Songs of Gods, Songs of Humans'. Her name Ape-huci / Kamuy-huci ('fire-grandmother') is uniform across Hokkaidō and Sakhalin Ainu.

figures.name: Kim-un-kamuy (the Bear, God of the Mountains)
figures.gloss: 'The one who is in the mountains' — the brown bear, ranking high among the kamuy as lord of the inland forests and master of the land-game. He is not merely a powerful animal but a mountain-kamuy who has dressed in a bear's body to walk among humans; the meat and pelt he leaves are gifts, and the hunt is the visit's purpose. Within the human village his cub is raised as an honoured guest.
figures.facet: The keeper of the wild is himself the most prized quarry — to kill the bear is not to defeat the master of animals but to receive him and host him home. The 'guest who is also the feast' folds predator, deity and game into a single reciprocal exchange found nowhere else in this map.
figures.provenance: Munro; Batchelor, 'The Ainu and Their Folk-Lore'; the bear's centrality is attested across all Ainu groups and is the focus of the iyomante ceremony.

figures.name: Repun-kamuy (the Orca, God of the Open Sea)
figures.gloss: 'The kamuy of the offing,' the killer-whale who lords the deep water as the bear lords the mountains, driving fish toward the shore and beaching whales for the villages. In the yukar he harpoons a whale and her calf and throws them ashore as a gift; told by a sea-wren that the humans butcher them roughly, he only laughs — the meat is theirs now. Stranded orcas were given true funerals, sent home like any kamuy.
figures.facet: The sea's master is a hunter who shares his kill upward with humankind, the opposite of a withholding sea-mother: abundance arrives as the orca's largesse, and his serenity at human disrespect makes the gift unconditional. The deep personified as generosity rather than as a grievance to be appeased.
figures.provenance: Rep-un-kamuy is widely attested; the whale-and-calf gift and the sea-wren are recorded in the yukar corpus (Philippi). The Ainu orca-funeral practice (e.g. on Rebun) is ethnographically documented.

figures.name: Kotan-kar-kamuy (the World-Shaper)
figures.gloss: 'The kamuy who makes the land/village' — the creator who shaped Ainu Mosir, the floating human world, dividing the waters from the dry ground and fitting the land for living. In some tellings he descends to do the shaping and then withdraws; the made world is left in the hands of the working kamuy of fire, mountain and sea who deal with humans daily.
figures.facet: A maker known almost entirely by his deed and then his absence — Ainu cosmology invests its devotion not in the distant world-shaper but in the near, embodied guests, so the creator recedes precisely as the hearth-grandmother and the bear come forward. Creation as a finished gift, not an ongoing reign.
figures.provenance: Reconstructed from scattered oshikep / creation narratives; the world-shaper's name and role vary across regions and are less uniformly attested than the working kamuy. Flagged as partly reconstructed (Munro, Philippi, Chiri Mashiho's collections).

figures.name: Aeoina-kamuy (Okikurmi, the Culture-Hero)
figures.gloss: The divine teacher who came down to Ainu Mosir and taught the first people everything human: how to make and keep fire, to hunt and fish, to build the house, to weave and sew, to brew, to speak and to pray rightly. Half-kamuy, half-of-the-people, he is the founder of Ainu custom; his very name, Ae-oina ('the one we recount / are taught of'), names the act of telling his deeds.
figures.facet: The culture-hero whose name IS narrative — to teach the arts and to be the subject of the oina (sacred tale) are one thing here, so civilisation and the telling of how it began are inseparable. He gives no stolen fire against the gods' will; he is sent to instruct, the gift licensed from above.
figures.provenance: Aeoina-kamuy / Okikurmi is the central culture-hero of the oina and yukar (Philippi groups these as 'Songs of Gods'); identification of Aeoina with Okikurmi is standard. Some traditions distinguish the two; flagged.

figures.name: Iyomante (the Bear-Sending)
figures.gloss: The great bear ceremony: a cub, taken young and raised in the village — at first suckled, treated as a beloved guest — is, after a year or two, honoured, feasted, and then ritually killed so that the mountain-kamuy wearing its body may be sent home laden with inau (prayer-sticks), sake and dumplings. He returns to the land of the kamuy praising his hosts, and so resolves to come again, bringing more game.
figures.facet: Killing reframed as hospitality completed — the slaying is the moment of release, the guest dismissed with gifts so richly that he will return. The 'send the spirit home' ethic in its most deliberate, year-long form: the prey is not consumed but escorted, and the future hunt is secured by present courtesy.
figures.provenance: Iyomante is the most thoroughly documented Ainu rite (Munro devotes much of 'Ainu Creed and Cult' to it; Batchelor; Piłsudski filmed/recorded Sakhalin variants). Now rare and ritually restricted.

figures.name: Ramat (soul / spirit-energy)
figures.gloss: The animating spirit-substance present in everything — people, bears, owls, fire, the cooking-pot, the mountain, the worn-out tool. Ramat is what makes a thing a someone; it can leave and pass on, can be honoured or offended, and is what survives when a body is used up. To break a vessel can be to free its ramat to serve in the next world; to disrespect a thing is to wrong the ramat in it.
figures.facet: The single principle under the whole crowded pantheon: there is no inert matter, only spirit thickly or thinly housed, so 'the many kamuy' are simply ramat at full intensity. Divinity is not a class of beings but a degree of indwelling life shared by gods, animals and pots alike.
figures.provenance: Ramat (ramat / ramachi) is the foundational concept in Munro's analysis of Ainu animism; corroborated by Batchelor and Sakhalin material from Piłsudski.

figures.name: Yukar (the epic chants)
figures.gloss: The vast oral epics, chanted to the beat of a stick on the hearth-edge, in which the kamuy and heroes tell their own stories — most often in the FIRST person, the god or hero narrating his own deeds through the reciter's mouth. The oina (sacred tales of Aeoina and the great kamuy) and the heroic yukar of Poiyaunpe together carry the whole theology; the Ainu, having no script, kept their gods alive entirely in performed voice.
figures.facet: Scripture sung in the god's own 'I' — the kamuy is not described from outside but lent a living human throat to speak through, so each recitation is a small possession, the divine biography re-uttered in the first person. The tradition's memory is a voice, never a page.
figures.provenance: Yukar and oina recorded by Kindaichi Kyōsuke, Chiri Yukie ('Ainu Shin'yōshū', the kamuy-yukar of the gods), and Philippi; Piłsudski recorded Sakhalin chants on wax cylinders. The first-person convention is a defining formal feature.

figures.name: Pase-kamuy (the Heavy Kamuy)
figures.gloss: The 'heavy' or weighty kamuy — the rank-term for the great, high gods (the fire-grandmother, the bear, the sea-orca, the world-shaper) as against the yayan ('ordinary') kamuy of lesser things. Weight here is gravity of importance and power: the pase-kamuy are those whose goodwill the village most depends on, addressed with the fullest ritual.
figures.facet: Hierarchy measured as weight, not height — the gods are ranked by how heavily they bear on human survival rather than by a throne or a sky-tier, an immanent ladder where the most 'important' kamuy is the one nearest your hearth and your hunt.
figures.provenance: The pase-kamuy / yayan-kamuy distinction is recorded by Munro and Batchelor; the precise membership of the 'heavy' rank varies by locality. Flagged as variable.

edges.note: The bear cannot be sent home except through her flame: Kamuy-huci is the gate of the iyomante, receiving the prayers and conveying the freed kamuy upward — rite and fire-grandmother are functionally one mechanism within the tradition.
The mountain-kamuy in his bear-body IS the honoured guest the iyomante hosts and sends home; the deity and the ceremony define each other.
The paired masters of the two wild domains — bear of the mountains, orca of the open sea — between them owning all the game the Ainu live by, land and water.
The 'heavy' kamuy are simply ramat at its fullest intensity: pase-kamuy names rank, ramat names the substance that is being ranked — the principle and its gradation.
Aeoina-kamuy is the founding subject of the oina, and his name ('the one we are taught of') names the chant itself — the culture-hero and the genre that carries him are bound at the root.
The two poles of Ainu divinity: the world-shaper who makes once and recedes, against the fire-grandmother who is present at every hearth every day — the withdrawn maker and the ever-near intercessor.

Both are the sacred hearth/altar fire that carries human offerings to the gods — the fire as messenger and mouth of sacrifice. Kamuy-huci diverges as an aged grandmother rather than a youthful priest-god, and stands at a wholly separate, non-Indo-European root; convergent function, no descent.
Eldest and most relied-upon of the heavy kamuy: the fire-grandmother sits at the head of the pase-kamuy rank she also serves as their common gate.

Both are a household great-power imagined as an old woman tending the family's threshold of life; a loose North-East-Asian 'grandmother-deity' rhyme — one of the hearth-and-prayer, one of birth — flagged as speculative.

The mountain-bear is the keeper and owner of the land-game who looses it to hunters — a clear instance of the master-of-animals joint, with the Ainu twist that the keeper is himself the received guest.

Two North-East-Asian bear-cult survivals: the Korean she-bear who becomes the people's mother and the Ainu he-bear who is the mountain-god hosted and sent home. A shared archaic bear-reverence belt, parallel and not a proven common cult — and the Ainu are a distinct non-Yamato, non-Korean people.

Both are master-spirits who OWN the land-animals so that every kill is a transaction inside a web of consent; the Inuit game-masters and the Ainu mountain-bear share the ethic that the hunt is negotiated with an owner, not merely taken.

Both preside over the wild and its game as mistress/master of animals; Artemis demands the hunter's respect and punishes the impious kill much as the bear-kamuy must be honoured — convergent keepers-of-the-wild across unrelated worlds.

A loose reach to Shiva as Pashupati, 'lord of beasts' — the master-of-animals role meets across vast distance, but the Ainu mountain-bear is a hunted guest, not a great ascetic god; flagged as speculative.

Same office, opposite valence: both are the master/mistress of the sea-game, but Sedna WITHHOLDS the seals in resentment until appeased, while Repun-kamuy freely drives the whales ashore and laughs off disrespect — the sea as grievance versus the sea as largesse.

Repun-kamuy is the open sea and its whales personified as a single ruling power — an instance of the mother-of-waters joint, diverging hard as a hunting orca who shares his kill upward with humankind rather than a primordial sea-parent or a withholding deep.

Two North-Pacific lords of the sea who send the deep's wealth and game to coastal villages; the undersea-chief of the Haida/Tlingit and the orca-kamuy of the Ainu rhyme as bestowers of marine abundance, parallel and unconnected.
The world-shaper who forms Ainu Mosir and then recedes, leaving the lived world to the working kamuy, is a clear deus-otiosus instance of the withdrawn-creator joint.
Both a creator who finishes the world and steps back from daily dealing with humankind; a loose withdrawn-maker parallel across wholly separate traditions, flagged.
Aeoina-kamuy / Okikurmi is the fire-, hunting-, weaving- and language-bringer who founds human custom — the culture-hero joint in full.

Both bring fire and the civilised arts to humankind, but inverted in licence: Prometheus STEALS fire against the gods' will, while Aeoina is SENT down to instruct — the culture-hero as sanctioned teacher versus stolen transgression.

Both are the founding culture-hero inseparable from song: Väinämöinen sings the world into order and Aeoina is the very subject of the oina chanted on the hearth — civilisation and its sung telling fused in one figure. Parallel, no contact.
The bear-sending is the master-of-animals ethic enacted as ceremony — by honouring and escorting the owner of the game home, the rite secures his return and the future hunt.

Both encode the 'return-the-game' covenant: as the salmon follow Fog Woman back to the sea when slighted, the bear-kamuy comes again only if sent home with proper respect — subsistence held by the keeping of courtesy to the animal-giver.

A loose reach: both make the slain great-animal the centre of sacred attention and the medium of right relation with the spirit-world, but the |Xam eland is a trance-and-potency animal while the Ainu bear is a hosted guest sent home — flagged as speculative.
Ramat is the single spirit-substance thickly or thinly housed in every being and object — the one-through-many joint stated as animism's bedrock, the unity beneath the crowded host of kamuy.

Both name a pervasive spiritual force/potency present in all things and persons, the animate charge under the visible world; ramat diverges by being the very soul that can leave a body and pass on, where orenda is more the power a being exerts. Parallel animisms, unconnected.

Both are the detachable soul that animates a being and can depart or be freed at death; the Inuit tarniq and Ainu ramat are kindred North-Pacific soul-concepts, structurally close, no descent claimed.

A speculative reach: both locate spirit/awareness as a substance pervading the whole world rather than a class of beings, but the Inuit Sila is breath-weather-sentience encompassing all, where ramat is the indwelling soul each thing individually carries — flagged as loose.

A loose parallel between two great oral-epic traditions of the North — the chanted yukar and the runo-song world of the Kalevala that Väinämöinen embodies; both keep the gods alive in performed voice without script. Flagged as a reach between a genre and a figure.
